data.title: Asia stocks extend slump as Greenland tensions and Wall Street sell-off weigh
data.description:
Asia stock markets were mostly lower on Wednesday, tracking firmer U.S. futures after Wall Street’s major benchmarks suffered their steepest one-day drop since October 10, as renewed tariff threats from President Donald Trump against Europe stoked volatility concerns. 

Trump threatened tariffs on eight European countries over Greenland, prompting concerns that European funds might offload US assets in retaliation.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slid nearly 5% to below $89,000 on Jan. 20, wiping out year-to-date gains and tracking a broad risk-off move across global markets as heightened tensions between the US and Europe.

Gold extended its bullish run above $4,800 per ounce on Wednesday, setting a fresh record.

The South Korean won advanced to around 1,471 per dollar, rebounding sharply from a four-week low after investor sentiment improved on government assurances. South Korea’s exports grew 14.9% year-on-year to USD 36.36 billion in the first 20 days of January 2026, data from the Korea Customs Service showed on Wednesday. 

Japan (NKY:IND) slid 0.56%. The Japanese yen held steady around 158 per dollar on Wednesday but faced downward pressure after Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s proposal to cut the 8% sales tax on food raised concerns about the fiscal outlook.

Japan logged its highest-ever December tourist arrivals at 3.6 million in 2025, pushing total arrivals for the year to a historic 42.7 million and above the 40-million mark for the first time. 

Investors also eyed the Bank of Japan’s policy meeting later this week, where rates are widely expected to stay unchanged following a December hike.

 Meanwhile, investors are bracing for a snap election on Feb. 8, as Takaichi seeks to consolidate power and advance her expansionary fiscal agenda. 

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.16% to above 4,120, while the Shenzhen Component gained 1% to 14,300 on Wednesday, reversing losses from the previous session as investor sentiment in China improved, and the offshore yuan weakened to around 6.96 per dollar on Wednesday, snapping a two-session gain as the People’s Bank of China kept the daily fixing weak.

On the corporate front, China Vanke bondholders approved a plan to defer certain yuan repayments by one year, according to a filing. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.19% to 26,518 in Wednesday morning trade, attempting to break a four-session losing streak. 

India (SENSEX) fell 0.41% to 81,681 in morning trade on Wednesday, retreating for a third straight session. The Indian rupee fell toward 91.3 per dollar on Wednesday, hitting an all-time low amid persistent foreign capital outflows and heightened geopolitical uncertainty.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.39% to around 8,780 on Wednesday, extending losses for a third consecutive session. The Australian dollar held its recent gains to around $0.673 on Wednesday, trading near a two-week high.

The Westpac–Melbourne Institute Leading Economic Index for Australia edged up 0.1% month-on-month in December 2025, following a flat reading in the previous month. 

In the U.S. on Tuesday, all three major indexes ended deep in red, with the S&P 500 down 1.9%, the Dow dropped 1.7%, and the Nasdaq sliding 1.9%. This was due to increased trade risks from President Trump's tariff threats.

U.S. stock futures steadied on Wednesday after the major averages slumped in the previous session: Dow +0.18%; S&P 500 +0.29%; Nasdaq +0.27%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Trump's Air Force One bound for Davos takes a U-turn
data.description:
The Air Force One plane carrying U.S. President Donald Trump to the World Economic Forum in Switzerland was forced to turn back late Tuesday due to what the White House called a “minor electrical issue” shortly after takeoff from Joint Base Andrews.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters aboard that the crew identified a minor electrical issue after takeoff and prudently decided to turn around and return to the base.

Air Force One landed back at the base at 11:07 p.m. ET. The president and those accompanying him will board a new aircraft and resume the trip to Switzerland, Leavitt said.

The technical glitch will likely delay Trump’s highly anticipated speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, scheduled at 8:30 a.m. ET Wednesday, shortly after landing.

The reporters aboard said that the lights in the press cabin briefly went out after takeoff, but no explanation was offered.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Wednesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes. The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders. This is a leading indicator for single-family home sales and housing construction.

8:30 AM Housing Starts and Permits

Housing starts measure the initial construction of single-family and multi-family units on a monthly basis. 

10:00 AM Construction Spending

The dollar value of new construction activity on residential, non-residential, and public projects. Data are available in nominal and real (inflation-adjusted) dollars.

Forecasters a decline of 0.1 percent on the month, maintaining continuing sluggishness in the sector.

10:00 AM Pending Home Sales Index

The National Association of Realtors developed the pending home sales index as a leading indicator of housing activity. A pending sale is one in which a contract was signed, but not yet closed. It usually takes four to six weeks to close a contracted sale.

1:00 PM 20-Yr Bond Auction

Treasury bonds are sold at regularly scheduled public auctions. The competitive bids at these auctions determine the interest rate paid on each Treasury note issue.

The US Treasury Department will hold an auction for $13 billion of 20-year bonds.

data.title: Cashmere Valley Bank GAAP EPS of $1.81, revenue of $23.3M
data.description:
• Cashmere Valley Bank press release (CSHX): Q4 GAAP EPS of $1.81.
  • Revenue of $23.3M (+9.1% Y/Y).

  • Return on equity decreased from 12.93% in fiscal 2024 to 11.76% for fiscal 2025. The decrease in return on equity was due to an increasing capital base from earnings and a reduction in unrealized losses on available for sale securities.

  • Return on assets decreased 2 basis points to 1.34%. While earnings held steady, asset growth occurred during the second half of the year resulting in the reduction.

  • The Bank's net interest margin increased seven basis points to 3.21% in 2025 as compared to 3.14% a year ago. Yield on earning assets increased six basis points while cost of funds fell by one basis point.

  • The efficiency ratio remained strong and decreased from 57.0% to 56.6% in spite of escalating personnel costs.

data.title: Hancock Whitney outlines 5%–6% net interest income growth and mid-single digit loan expansion for 2026 amid bond portfolio restructuring
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hancock Whitney Corporation (HWC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Hairston, President and CEO, highlighted that "the fourth quarter of 2025 was a strong finish to a remarkable year," citing year-over-year improvement in EPS of 8%, PPNR growth of 6%, and a 12% increase in tangible book value per share. Hairston emphasized the company’s focus on growing the balance sheet and improving profitability for 2026, with plans to hire up to 50 additional revenue-generating associates.
  • Hairston announced the completion of a bond portfolio restructuring, expected to benefit net interest margin (NIM) by 7 basis points and improve EPS by $0.23 per share annually. He cited deposit growth of $620 million and a new 5% share buyback plan effective through the end of 2026.
  • Michael Achary, Senior EVP and CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter's earnings were $126 million or $1.49 per share compared to $127 million or again $1.49 per share in the third quarter." Achary noted PPNR at $174 million and an efficiency ratio of 54.9% for the quarter. He described well-controlled expenses and solid fee income performance, with fee income at $107 million in the fourth quarter.

Outlook

  • The company’s guidance for 2026 projects NII growth of 5%–6% from 2025, with modest NIM expansion supported by the bond portfolio restructuring. PPNR is guided to increase 4.5%–5.5%. The efficiency ratio is projected to range between 54% and 55%.
  • Loan growth for 2026 is expected in the mid-single digits compared to the previous year-end. The company anticipates deposit growth in the low single digits from 2025 levels.
  • Fee income is expected to be up between 4% and 5%, and expenses are projected to rise 5%–6%, inclusive of organic growth plan investments and the Stable Trust Company acquisition.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income increased 1% quarter-over-quarter, driven by favorable volume and mix for average earning assets and interest-bearing liabilities. NIM was down 1 basis point, with the decrease attributed to lower loan yields. The yield on the bond portfolio increased 6 basis points to 2.98%.
  • The company reported a $0 loss bond swap of $230 million that achieved a yield pickup of 45 basis points. The bond portfolio restructuring involved selling $1.5 billion of bonds at a 2.49% yield and reinvesting at 4.35%, projected to add $24 million to NII and $0.23 to EPS annually.
  • Criticized commercial loans declined by $14 million to $535 million, and nonaccrual loans dropped by $7 million to $107 million. Net charge-offs were reported at 22 basis points. The allowance for loan losses stood at 1.43% of loans.
  • The company exhausted its previous share buyback authority, repurchasing about $147 million of shares in Q4 2025. The Board approved a new 5% repurchase plan for 2026.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James, asked about expectations for loan production versus paydowns. D. Loper, COO, described $1.6 billion of loan production in the quarter, with growth across Texas, Louisiana, and Florida, and shared that "our goal is to be the best bank for privately owned businesses in the country."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW, inquired about margin expansion beyond the bond restructuring. CFO Achary explained that "the main underpinnings...are really around the balance sheet," citing organic growth, improved securities yield, and reduced deposit costs.
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research, questioned the fee income guidance. Loper attributed confidence in the 4%–5% target to growth in consumer DDAs, mobile account openings, and business banking, while Achary noted the unpredictability of specialty fee lines.
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group, asked about the impact of security purchases on portfolio duration. Achary confirmed the purchases were predominantly commercial mortgage-backed securities and did not change portfolio duration.
  • Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi, questioned expectations for new banker performance and deposit gathering. Loper stated median breakeven is typically at 24–26 months, and both loans and deposits are emphasized for new hires.
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson, asked about NIM improvement and buyback pace. Achary clarified the 12–15 basis point NIM improvement is a Q4-to-Q4 metric and said buybacks will be more evenly distributed over 2026.
  • Christopher Marinac, Janney, sought clarity on credit quality and reserves. Chief Credit Officer Ziluca described the portfolio as resilient, with specific reserves already in place, and Achary noted an opportunity to modestly lower reserves but not below 125–130 basis points.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking details on growth expectations, margin expansion, and risk management, while pressing on sustainability of fee income and loan growth plans.
  • Management’s tone remained confident in prepared remarks and during Q&A, with frequent references to discipline and strong processes. Phrases like "we are confident..." and explanations of strategic investments underscored their positive outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence in growth initiatives and margin improvement was more pronounced, and analysts focused more on sustainability and execution of these plans.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with mid-single-digit loan growth and 5%–6% NII growth versus the previous quarter’s low-single-digit loan growth outlook.
  • The bond portfolio restructuring and its explicit contribution to NIM and EPS are new strategic developments.
  • Management’s tone has shifted to greater optimism about capital deployment, hiring, and organic growth. Analysts’ questions shifted from macroeconomic headwinds and paydown pressures to the sustainability of aggressive hiring and fee income guidance.
  • Key metrics such as criticized loan and nonaccrual loan levels continued to improve quarter-over-quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted continued competitive hiring for bankers and the challenge of generating full-relationship business in a tight labor market.
  • There is acknowledgment of paydown headwinds in CRE and competitive deposit pricing.
  • Analysts probed the unpredictability of specialty fee categories and the sustainability of fee income growth.
  • The company remains watchful of credit quality, with reserves maintained at conservative levels, and management is cautious about reducing reserves below certain thresholds.

Final Takeaway

Hancock Whitney management highlighted a strong finish to 2025 and outlined ambitious growth plans for 2026, including mid-single-digit loan and low single-digit deposit growth, supported by expanded hiring and a completed bond portfolio restructuring. The company projects NII growth of 5%–6% and expects positive contributions to NIM and EPS from the restructuring, while maintaining disciplined expense management and a new share buyback program. Management expressed confidence in their organic growth strategy and ability to deliver increased profitability and balance sheet expansion through targeted investments and operational discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zions Bancorporation signals potential capital distribution increase in 2026 as tangible book value rises 21%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zions Bancorporation (ZION) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Harris Simmons, Chairman & CEO, stated, "our fourth quarter results reflected continued progress and steady improvement across a variety of key financial metrics. Earnings of $262 million were up meaningfully, 19% from the prior quarter and 31% from a year ago, driven by stronger revenues and notably lower provision for credit losses." Simmons highlighted net interest margin expansion for the eighth consecutive quarter to 3.31%, with customer deposits growing at a 9% annualized pace, and period-end loan balances increasing by $615 million on solid production. He added, "Credit quality was strong with net charge-offs of just 5 basis points annualized of total loans."
  • Simmons noted a $15 million charitable donation impacting results due to recent tax law changes, while also referencing a tangible book value per share increase of 21% for the year and signaling that "we are nearing the point where we'll be able to increase capital distributions while continuing to further strengthen capital."
  • On EPS, Simmons reported, "diluted earnings per share was $1.76, up from $1.48 last quarter and $1.34 a year ago. This quarter's figure includes an $0.08 per share headwind from the charitable contribution, offset by a positive $0.11 per share combined impact from the reversal of the FDIC special assessment and net gains in our SBIC portfolio."
  • R. Richards, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net interest income increased by $56 million or 9% relative to the fourth quarter of 2024 and increased by $11 million relative to the prior quarter. For the second consecutive quarter, growth in average customer deposits in excess of loan growth aided our ability to improve funding mix and reduce overall funding costs. As a result, net interest margin expanded for the eighth consecutive quarter to 3.31%." Richards discussed a moderately increasing outlook for net interest income in 2026, supported by "favorable earning asset and interest-bearing liability remix in addition to growth in loans and deposits."

Outlook

  • Richards projected that net interest income for full-year 2026 is "moderately increasing relative to the full year of 2025, supported by favorable earning asset and interest-bearing liability remix in addition to growth in loans and deposits. Our guidance assumes 225 basis point cuts to the Fed funds rate occurring in June and September of this year."
  • Customer-related fee income is expected to be "moderately increasing relative to the full year 2025," with management currently expecting results "at the top end of that guide."
  • Loan balances for full-year 2026 are expected to be "moderately increasing relative to the full year of 2025 and assumes growth will be led by commercial loans, primarily in the C&I and owner-occupied subcategories with additional growth from commercial real estate loans."
  • Adjusted noninterest expense for 2026 is forecast as "moderately increasing relative to the full year of 2025," with ongoing investments in marketing, personnel, and technology. Richards added, "We continue to expect positive operating leverage in 2026 that we currently estimate around 100 to 150 basis points."

Financial Results

  • Simmons reported fourth quarter earnings of $262 million, net interest margin of 3.31%, customer deposits up 9% annualized, and diluted earnings per share of $1.76.
  • Richards detailed net interest income increase by $56 million versus Q4 2024, and by $11 million versus Q3 2025. Customer-related noninterest income reached $177 million for the quarter, with adjusted customer-related noninterest income at $175 million—a record for the company.
  • Adjusted noninterest expense was $548 million, including the $15 million charitable donation. When excluding the donation, expenses were up 2% versus the prior quarter.
  • Average loans were flat versus the previous quarter, but period-end balances increased by $615 million. Average noninterest-bearing deposits grew $1.7 billion or 6% from the prior quarter. Total funding costs declined by 16 basis points to 1.76%.
  • Credit quality remained solid, with net charge-offs at 5 basis points annualized and nonperforming assets at 52 basis points of loans and other real estate owned.
  • The common equity Tier 1 ratio for the quarter was 11.5%, and tangible book value per share grew 21% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarification on the expense base for guidance and the impact of the charitable contribution. Richards confirmed to "strip out the charitable contribution for this quarter and then rolling forward into next year."
  • Gosalia also asked about increased competition and marketing spend. Simmons responded, "that is not per se what is driving our focus on increased marketing spend. It's a revamp of some of our products...we're determined to actually spend more on growth initiatives."
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Inquired about NII outlook and funding mix. Richards explained, "we see the potential for remix on both sides of the balance sheet contributing to the NII outcome."
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: Asked about loan growth drivers. Simmons indicated focus on "small business lending," with SBA 7(a) loans nearly doubling in number and a 53% increase in dollars produced.
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Questioned timing and considerations for capital returns. Simmons stated, "I think it's probably this year, but not -- probably not this next quarter. In the second half, I think you'll see -- I would expect we're going to be in a position to start to accelerate capital returns."
  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: Asked about noninterest-bearing deposit trends. Simmons noted, "we've opened close to about 4,000 of them since we kind of relaunched this a few months ago."
  • Several analysts probed on operating leverage, expense growth, and NIM trajectory, with management emphasizing strategic spending and positive operating leverage expectations.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarification of expense and revenue bases, drivers of growth initiatives, and timing for capital returns, with a generally neutral and inquisitive tone, frequently seeking more detail on forward-looking strategies and risk controls.
  • Management maintained a confident and slightly optimistic tone, with Simmons and Richards emphasizing steady progress, robust deposit growth, and a determined focus on growth and efficiency initiatives. Simmons referenced, "we are nearing the point where we'll be able to increase capital distributions." Richards repeatedly highlighted positive operating leverage and moderated expense growth.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone was less dominated by credit event concerns and more oriented toward growth and capital strategies, while management’s tone was more assertive in outlining readiness for capital distribution and growth investments after a period of industry disruption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management shift from a defensive posture regarding isolated credit events in Q3 to a more proactive focus on growth, capital return, and technology initiatives in Q4.
  • Guidance language moved from "slightly to moderately increasing" loan growth in Q3 to "moderately increasing" in Q4, with increased emphasis on commercial and small business lending.
  • Net interest margin continued to expand, now at 3.31% compared to 3.28% in the prior quarter. Earnings and tangible book value growth accelerated, and management signaled more explicit intentions regarding capital returns.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from concerns over credit quality and one-off losses to questions about growth drivers, deposit initiatives, operating leverage, and timing of capital distributions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged ongoing competition in attractive markets but clarified increased marketing spend was tied to product revamps and growth initiatives rather than immediate competitive pressures.
  • Credit risk remained low, with net charge-offs minimal and classified loan balances declining in CRE but rising in C&I. Management expects further improvement in CRE classified balances through payoffs and upgrades.
  • Expense growth was discussed as a risk but was described as controlled through ongoing efficiency gains, technology investments, and outsourcing.
  • Interest rate risk is present, with deposit costs and NIM trajectory dependent on future rate changes and market behavior.

Final Takeaway

Zions Bancorporation closed 2025 with strong earnings momentum, an expanding margin, and robust deposit growth, while achieving a 21% increase in tangible book value per share. Management underscored a strategic pivot toward growth investments, particularly in small business lending and marketing initiatives, while maintaining prudent expense controls. With credit quality stable and capital ratios improving, leadership signaled readiness to accelerate capital distributions in the second half of 2026, positioning the bank for continued operating leverage and franchise growth in a changing rate environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Netflix forecasts $51B 2026 revenue and targets 31.5% operating margin as Warner Bros. acquisition advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Netflix (NFLX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Gregory Peters, Co-CEO, stated that Netflix delivered 16% revenue growth and roughly 30% operating profit growth in 2025, with expanding margins and growing free cash flow. "Ad sales, 2.5x in 2025. We expect that business to roughly double again in 2026 to about $3 billion." Peters emphasized organic progress and noted, "those goals were based on organic progress. They did not contemplate or assume any M&A."
  • Theodore Sarandos, Co-CEO, highlighted the focus for 2026 on "improving the core business" by increasing the variety and quality of content, enhancing the product experience, and strengthening the ad business. Sarandos announced, "We're working really hard to close the acquisition of Warner Bros. Studios and HBO, which we see as a strategic accelerant." He added, "We forecast 2026 revenue at $51 billion, which is up 14% year-on-year."
  • Spencer Neumann, CFO, reported, "We're targeting 31.5% operating margins for 2026. That's up 2 points. So no change to our approach there." He also noted a "rough doubling of our ad revenue in 2026 to about $3 billion."

Outlook

  • The company forecasts 2026 revenue at $51 billion, representing a 14% year-on-year increase, with an operating margin target of 31.5%. Management cited growth drivers as membership growth, pricing, and significant acceleration in ad revenue. Neumann said the company is "increasing our expense growth a bit this year, a bit higher pace of growth relative to last year to invest into those opportunities, all while continuing to expand our margins." Content amortization is expected to increase roughly 10% year-over-year, with a "content cash to expense ratio...steady at about the 1.1x ratio."
  • The strategic focus includes expanding investments in new content categories, such as video podcasts and cloud-first gaming, and closing the acquisition of Warner Bros. Studios and HBO.

Financial Results

  • Peters reported "16% revenue growth, roughly 30% operating profit growth, expanding margins, growing free cash flow" for 2025. Ad sales revenue grew 2.5x in 2025 and is forecast to double again in 2026. Total view hours in the second half of 2025 grew 2% year-on-year, with branded originals up 9% year-over-year in the same period.
  • CFO Neumann stated operating margins are targeted to grow by two percentage points, with margin expansion to continue despite increased investment, including about "0.5 percentage point drag from the expected M&A expenses."
  • Management highlighted a healthy lineup of returning and new series, as well as expansion in licensed content and live events.

Q&A

  • Robert Fishman, MoffettNathanson: Asked about long-term growth targets and whether they include M&A. Peters responded, "those goals were based on organic progress...we still feel good about those targets."
  • Steve Cahall, Wells Fargo: Inquired about content amortization growth and areas of incremental investment. Neumann said, "content amortization to increase roughly 10% year-over-year...content cash to expense ratio should hold pretty steady."
  • John Blackledge, TD Cowen: Asked for key drivers of 2026 revenue guidance and operating margin. Neumann cited membership growth, pricing, and ad revenue, reiterating the "31.5% operating margin" target.
  • Ben Swinburne, Morgan Stanley: Questioned if the Warner Bros. acquisition reflects a need to address stagnant engagement. Peters replied, "total view hours by itself, it's an overly simplified view into engagement and engagement trends...our retention is among the best in the industry, customer SAT at an all-time high."
  • Rich Greenfield, LightShed Partners: Asked about pricing strategy during the Warner Bros. deal process. Peters confirmed, "There is no impact or change to our approach and how we're running the business in that regard."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions regarding the sustainability of growth, engagement quality, and the impact of Warner Bros. acquisition, with a generally neutral tone, pressing for clarity on strategy and financial targets.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, frequently citing strong performance metrics and growth opportunities. Sarandos stated, "We're very excited about the business. We're very excited about the organic opportunity ahead."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted more toward probing the rationale and risks of the Warner Bros. acquisition, while management's confidence remained strong, bolstered by recent financial performance and guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter introduced the forecast of $51 billion in 2026 revenue and detailed the doubling of ad revenue to $3 billion, compared to prior quarter's focus on record share of TV time and ad revenue growth. The Warner Bros. acquisition was a new major strategic highlight.
  • Guidance language became more specific, with explicit margin and content amortization targets. Management maintained a positive tone both quarters but provided greater detail on forward-looking investments and integration plans this quarter.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from broad health and competitive positioning to deeper questions on M&A, engagement, and monetization strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged "a lot of hard work ahead to fully realize those opportunities, both short term and long term."
  • Regulatory approval for the Warner Bros. acquisition was cited as a process underway, with Sarandos stating, "We're confident we're going to be able to secure all the approvals because this deal is pro-consumer, it is pro-innovation, it's pro-worker, it is pro-creator and it is pro-growth."
  • Increased investment and M&A expenses are acknowledged as potential drags on margins, with Neumann referencing a "0.5 percentage point drag from the expected M&A expenses."

Final Takeaway

Netflix leadership emphasized continued strong organic growth, a robust content slate, and significant ad revenue acceleration, while the pending Warner Bros. and HBO acquisition is positioned as a strategic accelerator rather than a shift in core business direction. With 2026 revenue forecast at $51 billion and a 31.5% operating margin target, management remains confident in its ability to expand market share and profitability, supported by investments in content, technology, and new entertainment formats.
